parents.father_research_notes: Eleazier Curtis, descended from Thomas Curtis of Wethersfield Conn. through grandfather Zebulon Curtis (son of Thomas Curtis of Farmington, captain under Col. Wolcott, m. Mary Goodrich 1703). Father of Daniel B. Oviatt of Milford Conn. line per Whittlesey #826.
parents.mother_research_notes: Amanda Steele of New Hartford Conn., descended from John Steele of Hartford and Governor William Bradford of Plymouth Mass. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #826.

career.notes: Twelve-year pastorate; 'his health failing, he made a short tour abroad — in England, Scotland, and the continent.'
Resided in Hartford Conn. and engaged in preaching and literary work.

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from Governor Bradford and John Steele
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius's mother Amanda Steele of New Hartford Connecticut traced descent from John Steele of Hartford (one of the original Hartford settlers under Hooker) and Governor William Bradford of Plymouth Massachusetts. He himself descended from Thomas Curtis of Wethersfield through his grandfather Zebulon Curtis.

research_sources.page: Entry 826 (under Emily Chauncey Whittlesey, her husband)

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from Governor Bradford and John Steele
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius's mother Amanda Steele of New Hartford Connecticut traced descent from John Steele of Hartford (one of the original Hartford settlers under Hooker) and Governor William Bradford of Plymouth Massachusetts. research_sources.page: Entry 826 (under Emily Chauncey Whittlesey, her husband)

narrative_blocks.header: A soul of fidelity, courage, and religious devotion
narrative_blocks.body: Emily was praised in the Whittlesey Memorials as possessing 'a soul of fidelity, courage, religious strength, and devotion that has few equals.' She married Rev. Lucius Curtis on June 28, 1848 and accompanied his ministry across Connecticut, Wisconsin, and Iowa, never having children.

INST043.name: Williams College
INST043.notes: Private liberal arts college founded 1793 with funds from the estate of Ephraim Williams, a colonist killed in the French and Indian War (1755). One of the original "Little Three" liberal arts colleges.

INST121.primary_name: Yale Divinity School (Theological Department of Yale College / 'New Haven Seminary')
INST121.name: Yale Divinity School (Theological Department of Yale College / 'New Haven Seminary')
INST121.notes:
Founded 1822 as the Theological Department of Yale College; informally called 'New Haven Seminary' by 19th century pastors. Renamed Yale Divinity School in 1869.

INST137.name: Andover Theological Seminary
alt_names: Andover Newton Theological School (post-1908 merger with Newton Theological Institution)
INST137.notes: Founded 1807 as a conservative Trinitarian Congregational response to Unitarian Harvard. The most prestigious Protestant theological seminary in antebellum America. Trained ministers including Rev. John Ripley Adams (X02847, 1826), Rev. Edward Strong Dwight (HD1983), and many ABCFM missionaries. Merged with Newton Theological Institution 1908 to form Andover Newton Theological School; relocated to Newton MA. Now affiliated with Yale Divinity School (as of 2017). | Merged from INST050: Founded 1808 in Andover MA. Leading Calvinist seminary. Notable Hooker-tree connection: Rev. John Erskine Edwards (HD2631) graduated 1835.
